title: Hell & Judgment
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Hell and Judgment
summary: Scripture teaches that God is both merciful and just, that judgment is real, and that the severity of hell reflects the gravity of rejecting an infinitely good God.
body:
Jesus speaks about hell more than anyone else in Scripture. He uses the word Gehenna — a reference to the Valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, associated with burning refuse and child sacrifice — to describe a place where the fire does not go out [verse:mark.9.47-48]. Whatever hell ultimately is, Jesus treats it as real and terrible.

The parable of the sheep and goats presents judgment as the separation of those who served Christ in the vulnerable from those who ignored them [verse:matt.25.41-46]. The criteria are relational and ethical, not merely doctrinal. Jesus frames hell not as God's desire but as the consequence of choices made and compassion withheld.

Paul speaks of judgment with equal seriousness. God will judge each person's work [verse:rom.2.5-6]. The wrath of God is revealed against all godlessness and wickedness [verse:rom.1.18]. Yet Paul's entire ministry is driven by the conviction that this judgment has been absorbed by Christ for all who believe.

Peter echoes this tension: God is patient, not wanting anyone to perish but everyone to come to repentance [verse:2pet.3.9]. Judgment is real, but it is not God's preference. The door remains open.

Revelation depicts a final judgment where death and Hades are thrown into the lake of fire [verse:rev.20.14-15]. The imagery is severe — but so is the evil being judged. A God who shrugged at injustice, cruelty, and the destruction of his image-bearers would not be good. Judgment is what a just God does about evil.

verses.annotation: Jesus uses Gehenna imagery — unquenchable fire — to convey the severity of final separation from God.
The sheep and goats — judgment based on how people treated the vulnerable.
God's patience — he does not want anyone to perish but desires universal repentance.
God will render to each person according to their deeds — judgment is individual and real.
The wrath of God revealed against ungodliness — divine anger at genuine evil.
The lake of fire — Revelation's imagery for final, irreversible judgment.
God sent his Son not to condemn the world but to save it — judgment is the alternative to rescue.

scholars.quote: Keener argues that Jesus' Gehenna language was deliberately shocking, using the most disturbing image available in his culture to convey the seriousness of rejecting God — it was intended to warn, not to terrorize.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Moo reads Romans 1-2 as Paul establishing that every person stands justly condemned before presenting the gospel as rescue — the severity of judgment is the measure of the grace required to overcome it.

Beale interprets Revelation 20's judgment scene as the culmination of God's long patience — final judgment is not arbitrary but the just conclusion of a history in which every opportunity for repentance has been extended.
